slides.title: Functions Can Hand Things Back
slides.body: Built-in functions like `len()` and `round()` don't just *do* something — they **give you a result** you can use. The `return` statement is how any function sends data back to the caller.
slides.tip: Think of a vending machine: you press a button and it hands back a snack you can actually keep.

slides.title: Using return to Send a Value
slides.body: Write `return` followed by a value and Python exits the function immediately, handing that value back. You can store it in a variable and use it anywhere.
slides.code:
def add(a, b):
    return a + b

total = add(3, 5)
print(total)
slides.tip: The returned value is yours to store, pass on, or do maths with.

slides.title: return vs. print — Not the Same!
slides.body: `print` shows a value on screen and then it's **gone**. `return` hands the value back so your code can actually **use** it. A function with only `print` secretly returns `None`.
slides.code:
def double_print(x):
    print(x * 2)

a = double_print(4)
print(a)
slides.tip: If you try to do maths on the result of a print-only function, you'll get a TypeError.

slides.title: Exit Early with return
slides.body: `return` can appear anywhere in a function — the moment Python hits it, the function stops. This **early return** lets you handle edge cases cleanly without deep nesting.
slides.tip: Handle special cases first and return early — the main logic stays unindented and easy to read.

slides.title: Return Two Values at Once
slides.body: Separate values with a comma after `return` and Python packs them into a tuple. **Unpack** them into separate variables on the left side of `=` in one clean line.
slides.tip: You can also keep the result as a single tuple: result = min_max(nums).

slides.title: You've Got the Power to Return!
slides.body: `return` transforms a function from a one-way action into a **two-way conversation** — it does work and sends the answer back. No `return`? Python quietly hands back `None`.
slides.tip: Rule of thumb: if you want to use the result later, return it. If you only want to display it, print it.
